title: Myrtle Beach, South Carolina

infobox.value: The [wax myrtle](/wiki/Myrica "Myrica"), an abundant local shrub[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Members[[4]](#cite_note-Myrtle_Beach_Mayor_and_City_Council_Members-4)   * Mark Kruea – Mayor * Jackie Vereen Hatley – Mayor Pro Tempore * Michael Chestnut * John Krajc * Clyde H. "Mike" Lowder * Philip N. Render, DMD * Gregg Smith
[13th](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_South_Carolina "List of cities and towns in South Carolina") in South Carolina

is a [resort city](/wiki/Resort_city "Resort city") in [Horry County, South Carolina](/wiki/Horry_County,_South_Carolina "Horry County, South Carolina"), United States. It is located in the center of a long and continuous 60-mile (97 km) stretch of beach known as the "[Grand Strand](/wiki/Grand_Strand "Grand Strand")” in the northeastern part of the state, on the [East Coast of the United States](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States"). Its year-round population was 35,682 as of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), making it the [13th-most populous city in South Carolina](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_South_Carolina "List of municipalities in South Carolina").[[11]](#cite_note-2020CensusQuickFacts-11)

**Myrtle Beach** is a [resort city](/wiki/Resort_city "Resort city") in [Horry County, South Carolina](/wiki/Horry_County,_South_Carolina "Horry County, South Carolina"), United States. It is located in the center of a long and continuous 60-mile (97 km) stretch of beach known as the "[Grand Strand](/wiki/Grand_Strand "Grand Strand")” in the northeastern part of the state, on the [East Coast of the United States](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States"). Its year-round population was 35,682 as of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), making it the [13th-most populous city in South Carolina](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_South_Carolina "List of municipalities in South Carolina").[[11]](#cite_note-2020CensusQuickFacts-11)

Myrtle Beach is one of the major centers of tourism in [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). The city's warm [subtropical climate](/wiki/Subtropics "Subtropics"), miles of beaches, 86 golf courses, and 1,800 restaurants attract over 20 million visitors each year, making Myrtle Beach one of the most visited destinations in the country.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Located along the historic [King's Highway](/wiki/King%27s_Highway_(Charleston_to_Boston) "King's Highway (Charleston to Boston)") (modern day [U.S. Route 17](/wiki/U.S._Route_17_in_South_Carolina "U.S. Route 17 in South Carolina")), the region was once home to the [Waccamaw](/wiki/Waccamaw "Waccamaw") people. During the colonial period, the Whither family settled in the area, and a prominent local waterway, Wither's Swash, is named in their honor. Originally called alternately "New Town" or "Withers", the area was targeted for development as a resort community by [Franklin Burroughs](/wiki/Franklin_Burroughs_(businessman) "Franklin Burroughs (businessman)"), whose sons completed a railroad to the beach and the first inn, Seaside Inn. His widow named the new community Myrtle Beach after the local [wax-myrtle](/wiki/Myrica "Myrica") shrubs.

The [Myrtle Beach Metro Area](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_metropolitan_area "Myrtle Beach metropolitan area") is one of the fastest growing [metropolitan areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_areas "Metropolitan areas") in the country, with an estimated population of 479,426 in 2024.[[8]](#cite_note-PopEstCBSA-8) More than 104,000 people moved to the area over eight years, representing a nearly 28% growth in population.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/fb/FGBurroughs.jpg/250px-FGBurroughs.jpg)](/wiki/File:FGBurroughs.jpg)

The F.G. Burroughs steamship

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Mbaaf-1943-1.jpg/250px-Mbaaf-1943-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mbaaf-1943-1.jpg)

Original [Myrtle Beach Air Force Base](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_Air_Force_Base "Myrtle Beach Air Force Base") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Rainbow_Court_Myrtle_Beach_SC_Jun_10.JPG/250px-Rainbow_Court_Myrtle_Beach_SC_Jun_10.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rainbow_Court_Myrtle_Beach_SC_Jun_10.JPG)

[Rainbow Court](/wiki/Rainbow_Court "Rainbow Court") (built 1935 to 1959), now demolished, was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Horry_County,_South_Carolina "National Register of Historic Places listings in Horry County, South Carolina").

Prior to the arrival of Europeans, the [Long Bay](/wiki/Grand_Strand "Grand Strand") area was inhabited by the historic [Waccamaw](/wiki/Waccamaw "Waccamaw") tribe. The Waccamaw used the river for travel and fished along the shore around [Little River](/wiki/Little_River,_South_Carolina "Little River, South Carolina"). [Waties Island](/wiki/Waties_Island,_South_Carolina "Waties Island, South Carolina"), the primary [barrier island](/wiki/Barrier_island "Barrier island") along Long Bay, has evidence of burial and shell mounds, remains of the visiting Waccamaw.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The first European settlers along Long Bay arrived in the late 18th century, attempting to extend the [plantation system](/wiki/Plantation_economy "Plantation economy") outward toward the ocean.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Records are sparse from this period, with most of the recorded history pieced together from English colonial [land grant](/wiki/Land_grant "Land grant") documents. These settlers gained mixed results, producing unremarkable quantities of [indigo](/wiki/Indigo "Indigo") and tobacco, the two major commodity crops. The coast's soil was sandy and most of the crop yields were of inferior quality.

Prior to the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), the area along the future Grand Strand was essentially uninhabited. Several families received land grants along the coast, including the Witherses: John, Richard, William, and Mary. This family received an area around present-day Wither's Swash, also known as Myrtle Swash or the Eight-Mile Swash. A separate grant was granted to James Minor, including a barrier island named Minor Island, now [Waties Island](/wiki/Waties_Island,_South_Carolina "Waties Island, South Carolina"), off the coast near Little River.[[18]](#cite_note-Bedford-18): 36

Mary Withers's gravestone at [Prince George Winyah Parish Church](/wiki/Prince_George_Winyah_Parish_Church "Prince George Winyah Parish Church") speaks to the remoteness of the former Strand: "She gave up the pleasures of Society and retired to Long Bay, where she resided a great part of her life devoted to the welfare of her children."[[19]](#cite_note-19)

As the American colonies gained independence, the area remained essentially unchanged, and the coast remained barren. [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") scouted out the Southern states during his term, traveling down the [King's Highway](/wiki/King%27s_Highway_(Charleston_to_Boston) "King's Highway (Charleston to Boston)"). He stayed a night at [Windy Hill](/wiki/Windy_Hill_Beach "Windy Hill Beach") (part of present-day [North Myrtle Beach](/wiki/North_Myrtle_Beach "North Myrtle Beach")) and was led across Wither's Swash to [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown,_South_Carolina "Georgetown, South Carolina") by Jeremiah Vereen.[[18]](#cite_note-Bedford-18): 51

The Withers family remained one of the few settlers around Myrtle Beach for the next half-century. In 1822, a strong [hurricane](/wiki/Hurricane "Hurricane") swept the house of R. F. Withers into the ocean, drowning 18 people inside. The tragedy made the Withers family decide to abandon their plots along the coast. Left unattended, the area began to return to forest.[[18]](#cite_note-Bedford-18): 58

The Burroughs and Collins Company of [Conway](/wiki/Conway,_South_Carolina "Conway, South Carolina"), predecessor of modern-day Burroughs & Chapin, purchased much of the Withers family's land in 1881. The growing community was called "New Town" around the start of the 20th century. A [post office](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") named "Withers" was established to serve the site of the old Swash in 1888. On 28 February 1899, Burroughs and Collins received a charter to build the Conway & Seashore Railroad to transport timber from the coast to inland customers. The railroad began daily service on 1 May 1900, with two wood-burning locomotives. One of the engines was dubbed *The Black Maria* and came second-hand from a [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina") logging operation.

After the railroad was finished, employees of the lumber and railroad company would take train [flatcars](/wiki/Flatcar "Flatcar") down to the beach area on their free weekends, becoming the first Grand Strand tourists.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The railroad terminus was nicknamed "New Town", contrasting it with the "Old Town", or [Conway](/wiki/Conway,_South_Carolina "Conway, South Carolina").

Around the start of the 20th century, [Franklin Burroughs](/wiki/Franklin_Burroughs_(businessman) "Franklin Burroughs (businessman)") envisioned turning New Town into a tourist destination rivaling the Florida and [northeastern](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States") beaches. Burroughs died in 1897, but his sons completed the railroad's expansion to the beach and opened the Seaside Inn in 1901.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Around 1900, a contest was held to name the area, and Burroughs's wife suggested honoring the locally abundant shrub, the southern wax myrtle (*[Myrica cerifera](/wiki/Myrica_cerifera "Myrica cerifera")*). The Withers post office changed its name to "Myrtle Beach" soon afterward. It incorporated as a town in 1938 and as a city in 1957.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In 1937, Myrtle Beach Municipal Airport was built. It was taken over by the [U.S. Army Air Corps](/wiki/United_States_Army_Air_Corps "United States Army Air Corps") in 1940 and converted into a [military base](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_Air_Force_Base "Myrtle Beach Air Force Base"). Commercial flights began in 1976 and shared the runway for over 15 years until the air base closed in 1993. Since then the airport has been named [Myrtle Beach International Airport](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_International_Airport "Myrtle Beach International Airport"). In 2010 plans to build a new terminal were approved. In 1940, Kings Highway was finally paved, giving Myrtle Beach its first primary highway.

The [Myrtle Heights-Oak Park Historic District](/wiki/Myrtle_Heights-Oak_Park_Historic_District "Myrtle Heights-Oak Park Historic District"), [Myrtle Beach Atlantic Coast Line Railroad Station](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_Atlantic_Coast_Line_Railroad_Station "Myrtle Beach Atlantic Coast Line Railroad Station"), [Ocean Forest Country Club](/wiki/Ocean_Forest_Country_Club "Ocean Forest Country Club") and [Pleasant Inn](/wiki/Pleasant_Inn "Pleasant Inn") are listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"). Also listed were the [Chesterfield Inn](/wiki/Chesterfield_Inn "Chesterfield Inn"),[[23]](#cite_note-nris-23) [Rainbow Court](/wiki/Rainbow_Court "Rainbow Court") and the [Myrtle Beach Pavilion](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_Pavilion "Myrtle Beach Pavilion"), all now demolished. The [Gay Dolphin Gift Cove](/wiki/Gay_Dolphin_Gift_Cove "Gay Dolphin Gift Cove") on the [Boardwalk](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_Boardwalk "Myrtle Beach Boardwalk") was built in 1946 and sells seashells and Myrtle Beach souvenirs. It claims to be the "nation's largest gift shop".

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Myrtle Beach has been separated from the continental United States since 1936 by the [Intracoastal Waterway](/wiki/Intracoastal_Waterway "Intracoastal Waterway"),[[24]](#cite_note-24) forcing the city and area in general to develop within a small distance from the coast. In part due to this separation, the area directly northwest of Myrtle Beach, across the waterway, remained primarily rural for a while, whereas its northeastern and southwestern ends were bordered by other developed tourist towns, [North Myrtle Beach](/wiki/North_Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina "North Myrtle Beach, South Carolina") and [Surfside Beach](/wiki/Surfside_Beach,_South_Carolina "Surfside Beach, South Carolina"). Since then, the inland portion of the Myrtle Beach area has developed dramatically.

Myrtle Beach is 67 miles (108 km) by highway southeast of [Florence, South Carolina](/wiki/Florence,_South_Carolina "Florence, South Carolina"), 94 miles (151 km) northeast of [Charleston, South Carolina](/wiki/Charleston,_South_Carolina "Charleston, South Carolina"), and 74 miles (119 km) southwest of [Wilmington, North Carolina](/wiki/Wilmington,_North_Carolina "Wilmington, North Carolina").

According to the United States Census Bureau, the city has a total area of 23.69 square miles (61.4 km2), of which 23.42 square miles (60.7 km2) is land and 0.27 square miles (0.70 km2) (1.14%) is water.[[5]](#cite_note-TigerWebMapServer-5)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

According to the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), Myrtle Beach has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") or Cfa – typical of the Gulf and South Atlantic states. The city enjoys abundant sunshine year-round with more than 2800 hours annually.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Beaches_of_Myrtle_Beach%2C_South_Carolina.jpg/250px-Beaches_of_Myrtle_Beach%2C_South_Carolina.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beaches_of_Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina.jpg)

Myrtle Beach is protected from erosion by vegetation-filled [sand dunes](/wiki/Sand_dunes "Sand dunes").

The summer season is long, hot, and humid in Myrtle Beach. Average daytime highs are from 83 to 91 °F (28 to 33 °C) and average night-time lows are near 70 °F (21 °C). The coastal location of Myrtle Beach mitigates daytime summer heat *somewhat* compared to inland areas of South Carolina: Thus, while nearby [Florence](/wiki/Florence,_South_Carolina "Florence, South Carolina") averages 65 days annually with high temperatures of 90 °F (32 °C) or higher – Myrtle Beach averages only 21. The [Bermuda High](/wiki/Bermuda_High "Bermuda High") pumps in humidity from the tropical Atlantic toward Myrtle Beach, giving summers a near-tropical feel in the city. The warm Atlantic Ocean reaches 80 °F (27 °C) or higher in the summer months off Myrtle Beach, making for warm and sultry summer nights. Summer thunderstorms are common in the hot season in Myrtle Beach, and the summer months from June through September have the most [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation "Precipitation"). In summer, thunderstorms normally build during the heat of the day – followed by brief and intense downpours. On average, September is the wettest month, with August close behind, due to the combination of thunderstorms and tropical weather systems that peaks these months.

Myrtle Beach has mostly mild winters of short duration: Average daytime highs range from 57 to 61 °F (14 to 16 °C) and nighttime lows are in the 36 to 38 °F (2 to 3 °C) from December through February. Winter temperatures vary more than summer temperatures in Myrtle Beach: Some winters can see several cold days with highs only in the upper 40s F (7–9 °C), while other winter days can see highs in the upper 60s and low 70s F (19–23 °C). Myrtle Beach averages 33 days annually with [frost](/wiki/Frost "Frost"), though in some years less than 15 days will see frost. Snowfall is very rare in Myrtle Beach; however, at least a trace of snow falls a few times each decade. In February 2010, a rare 2.8 inches (71 mm) of snow fell in Myrtle Beach.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) The spring (March, April and May) and fall (September, October and November) months are normally mild and sunny in Myrtle Beach, with high temperatures in the 60s and 70s. The beach season in Myrtle Beach normally runs from late April through late October. [Sea surface temperatures](/wiki/Sea_Surface_Temperature "Sea Surface Temperature") are often in the lower 80's (26–28 °C) off South Carolina in summer and early fall.

The temperature range measured in Myrtle Beach has historically ranged from a record high of 104 °F (40 °C) on June 26, 1952, and August 5, 1954, to the record winter cold of 9 °F (−13 °C) on February 18, 1958.[[27]](#cite_note-NOWData-27) In spite of the mild winter averages, ice days have been recorded, albeit rarely. The coldest daily maximum was 25 °F (−4 °C) on January 11, 1962, whereas the normal year between 1991 and 2020 had the coldest maximum average of 37 °F (3 °C).[[27]](#cite_note-NOWData-27) Overnight lows can be extremely hot and muggy, although the record warm low of 88 °F (31 °C) on July 12, 1942, is some way above the means of 81 °F (27 °C) for the warmest annual minimum.[[27]](#cite_note-NOWData-27)

Summer thunderstorms are typically brief, but severe thunderstorms do occur on occasion. Tornadoes are rare, with the most significant event occurring in [2001](/wiki/Tornadoes_of_2001#July_6 "Tornadoes of 2001") when multiple tornadoes touched down in the area. [Tropical cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone") occasionally impact Myrtle Beach, though weaker tropical storms and weak tropical lows are more common. Like most areas prone to tropical cyclones, a direct hit by a major hurricane is infrequent in Myrtle Beach. The last hurricane to cause significant damage in Myrtle Beach was [Hurricane Hugo](/wiki/Hurricane_Hugo "Hurricane Hugo") in 1989. The worst hurricane in Myrtle Beach's history was [Hurricane Hazel](/wiki/Hurricane_Hazel "Hurricane Hazel") in 1954.

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), there were 35,682 people, 16,759 households, and 7,678 families residing in the city.[[34]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-34)

The median age was 49.4 years, with 15.8% of residents under the age of 18 and 25.4% aged 65 years or older, and there were 97.5 males for every 100 females, dropping to 95.5 males for every 100 females age 18 and over.[[34]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-34)

Of those households, 18.4% had children under the age of 18 living in them, 40.2% were married-couple households, 22.4% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 30.3% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present; about 35.3% were made up of individuals and 15.0% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[34]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-34)

There were 22,456 housing units, of which 25.4% were vacant, with a homeowner vacancy rate of 2.9% and a rental vacancy rate of 10.9%.[[34]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-34)

100.0% of residents lived in urban areas, while 0.0% lived in rural areas.[[35]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-35)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Economy")]

Myrtle Beach's economy is dominated by the tourist industry;[[38]](#cite_note-38) hotels, motels, resorts, restaurants, attractions, and retail developments exist in abundance to serve visitors.

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Tourism")]

See also: [Economy of South Carolina](/wiki/Economy_of_South_Carolina#Lowcountry_tourism "Economy of South Carolina") and [List of attractions in Myrtle Beach, South Carolina](/wiki/List_of_attractions_in_Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina "List of attractions in Myrtle Beach, South Carolina")

Hosting over 20 million visitors annually,[[39]](#cite_note-:0-39) the Grand Strand is home to an array of tourist attractions, and the area receives a large influx of visitors during all seasons. It is estimated that nearly 100,000 visitors a year are international travelers, including tourists from Canada, Germany and the United Kingdom.[[40]](#cite_note-Myrtle_Beach_Hotels_and_Tourism-40)

[![Ocean Boulevard in Myrtle Beach](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Ocean-ave-myrtle.jpg/250px-Ocean-ave-myrtle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ocean-ave-myrtle.jpg)

Ocean Boulevard in Myrtle Beach

Myrtle Beach hosts a variety of special conventions, events, and musical concerts. The area's attractions include its beaches and courses, amusement parks, an aquarium, [Legends in Concert](/wiki/Legends_in_Concert "Legends in Concert"), retail developments, shopping complexes and over 1,900 restaurants[[41]](#cite_note-myrtlebeachareachamber.com-41) including seafood restaurants. The area also has dinner theaters, nightclubs, and many tourist shops. Myrtle Beach has approximately 425 hotels, with many on the beachfront, and approximately 157 accommodation units in total. The area is also home to over 80 golf courses and over 30 miniature golf courses.[[39]](#cite_note-:0-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Margate_Tower_6-7-17.png/250px-Margate_Tower_6-7-17.png)](/wiki/File:Margate_Tower_6-7-17.png)

The 29-floor Margate Tower in Kingston Plantation is the tallest building in the Myrtle Beach area.

Live shows are offered year round at theaters including the [Alabama Theatre](/wiki/Alabama_Theatre_(Myrtle_Beach) "Alabama Theatre (Myrtle Beach)"), [Carolina Opry](/wiki/Carolina_Opry "Carolina Opry"), Asher Theater, Calvin Gilmore Theater, [Legends in Concert](/wiki/Legends_in_Concert "Legends in Concert"), and [Medieval Times](/wiki/Medieval_Times "Medieval Times") Family Dinner Theater. Several shows offer special holiday themed shows.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The [Myrtle Beach Boardwalk](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_Boardwalk "Myrtle Beach Boardwalk") opened in 2010 and has been recognized as the nation's #3 boardwalk by *[National Geographic](/wiki/National_Geographic_Society "National Geographic Society")*[[43]](#cite_note-43) and one of the best US boardwalks by [Travel + Leisure](/wiki/Travel_%2B_Leisure "Travel + Leisure") magazine.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The Myrtle Beach Skywheel opened at the boardwalk in May 2011, and is a 200-foot (61 m) observation wheel, similar to a ferris wheel, with glass gondolas that look over the Atlantic Ocean. This is the first wheel of its kind in the U.S. [Myrtle Beach State Park](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_State_Park "Myrtle Beach State Park"), established in 1935, has just under a mile of Grand Strand beach and is a prime location for camping, swimming, hiking, biking, and fishing. #### Annual events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Annual events")]

Each March since 1951 during [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario")'s [spring break](/wiki/Spring_break "Spring break"), Myrtle Beach has hosted Canadian-American Days, also known as Can-Am Days. Tens of thousands of tourists flock to the area for a week's worth of special events.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Myrtle Beach is also home to Coastal Uncorked, a food and wine festival held in the late spring annually. The city hosts [Sun Fun Festival](/wiki/Sun_Fun_Festival "Sun Fun Festival") early each June.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Later in June, Myrtle Beach is a popular destination for recently graduated high school seniors for [Senior Week](/wiki/Senior_Week "Senior Week").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

[Carolina Country Music Fest](/wiki/Carolina_Country_Music_Fest "Carolina Country Music Fest") (CCMF) is an outdoor country music festival that takes place in June of each year and was founded in 2015 by Charlotte-based company Full House Productions. Due to concerns surrounding the coronavirus pandemic the festival wasn't held in 2020, but is scheduled to return in 2021 and take place on 10–13 June 2021.

[Myrtle Beach International Airport](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_International_Airport "Myrtle Beach International Airport") serves the city and surrounding area. With regular flights to and from destinations such as Atlanta, Boston, Detroit, Fort Lauderdale, New York and Washington, the airport is well connected for both domestic and international tourists. It also serves as a seasonal gateway to and from the likes of Chicago, Dallas and Toronto.

The city is between [Wilmington, North Carolina](/wiki/Wilmington,_North_Carolina "Wilmington, North Carolina") and [Charleston, South Carolina](/wiki/Charleston,_South_Carolina "Charleston, South Carolina") with [U.S. Route 17](/wiki/U.S._Route_17 "U.S. Route 17") serving as the main transport link for those journeying between those cities.[[40]](#cite_note-Myrtle_Beach_Hotels_and_Tourism-40)

#### Thong bikini ban

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Thong bikini ban")]

[Thongs](/wiki/Thong "Thong") (or any swimwear revealing any portion of the [buttocks](/wiki/Buttocks "Buttocks")) are not permitted in public in Myrtle Beach, including all beaches.[[48]](#cite_note-myrtle_beach_thong_ban-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) Violators of the ban may be arrested, jailed, or fined.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) The city's "thong ordinance" has been in effect since the 1990s.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

##### Motorcycle rallies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Motorcycle rallies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Yellow_and_black_and_silver_Suzukis_Black_Bike_Week_2008.jpg/250px-Yellow_and_black_and_silver_Suzukis_Black_Bike_Week_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yellow_and_black_and_silver_Suzukis_Black_Bike_Week_2008.jpg)

Riders in traffic at the 2008 [Atlantic Beach Bikefest](/wiki/Black_Bike_Week "Black Bike Week")

Myrtle Beach Bike Week, also called "Harley Bike Week", is a week-long [motorcycle rally](/wiki/Motorcycle_rally "Motorcycle rally") first held in 1940, the same year Kings Highway was paved.[[53]](#cite_note-53) The event has attracted as many as 200,000 visitors to the city every May. [Black Bike Week](/wiki/Black_Bike_Week "Black Bike Week"), founded in 1980, takes place the weekend around Memorial Day Weekend and is the largest African American motorcycle rally in the US and attracts as many as 400,000 visitors. The event was created in response to a history of discrimination against African-American visitors and riders to Myrtle Beach and the Grand Strand Area.[[54]](#cite_note-GroupLawsuits-54)[[55]](#cite_note-Gettleman_122-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)

The Myrtle Beach government created 15 new laws aimed at preventing all sanctioned motorcycle events within the city in response to controversy including accusations of racism by African-American riders during their event and complaints of lawlessness and poor behavior during all highly attended events. Several lawsuits by the [National Association for the Advancement of Colored People](/wiki/National_Association_for_the_Advancement_of_Colored_People "National Association for the Advancement of Colored People") (NAACP) against Myrtle Beach businesses were settled with agreements that discrimination cease, compensation be given to some plaintiffs, and employees be given diversity training.[[54]](#cite_note-GroupLawsuits-54)[[55]](#cite_note-Gettleman_122-55)[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) The NAACP suit against the City of Myrtle Beach was settled in 2006 without the city paying damages, but with the agreement police would use the same traffic control rules during both the black and the white motorcycle rallies.[[59]](#cite_note-MBrelease-59)

The [South Carolina Supreme Court](/wiki/South_Carolina_Supreme_Court "South Carolina Supreme Court") in June 2010 unanimously overturned one of the 15 ordinances, which had required all motorcyclists to wear [helmets](/wiki/Motorcycle_helmet "Motorcycle helmet"), on the grounds that the state law, requiring helmets only for riders under age 21, cannot be preempted by a city ordinance. In addition, the Court ruled that the ordinance created undue confusion, and that the city itself had invalidated their own helmet law and some other ordinances in a subsequent amendment.[[60]](#cite_note-Harley2010-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) The law had been challenged by a group of motorcyclists and a group of Myrtle Beach businesses called BOOST, Business Owners Organized to Support Tourism, who opposed the city's anti-motorcycle tourism policy.[[62]](#cite_note-TourismPolicy-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)

The [Alabama Theatre](/wiki/Alabama_Theatre_(Myrtle_Beach) "Alabama Theatre (Myrtle Beach)") at Barefoot Landing

Major shopping malls in Myrtle Beach include:

* [Barefoot Landing](/wiki/Barefoot_Landing "Barefoot Landing") is an outdoor shopping complex that consists of several divided sections on filled land over top of Louis Lake, next to the Intracoastal Waterway. It contains many stores and attractions such as [House of Blues](/wiki/House_of_Blues "House of Blues") (opened in 1997) and the [Alabama Theatre](/wiki/Alabama_Theatre_(Myrtle_Beach) "Alabama Theatre (Myrtle Beach)"). Opened in 1988.
* [Broadway at the Beach](/wiki/Broadway_at_the_Beach "Broadway at the Beach") is a shopping complex set on 350 acres (1.4 km2) along the [U.S. 17 Bypass](/wiki/U.S._Route_17_in_South_Carolina "U.S. Route 17 in South Carolina") surrounding the 23-acre (9.3 ha) Lake Broadway. It is the largest festival entertainment complex in South Carolina.[[66]](#cite_note-66)
* [Coastal Grand Mall](/wiki/Coastal_Grand_Mall "Coastal Grand Mall") opened in 2004 and is the second largest indoor malls in the state. The mall, which has indoor and outdoor shopping areas, has a gross leasable area of 1,047,732 square feet (97,300 m2). The single-story facility has five anchor stores (including [Sears](/wiki/Sears "Sears"), [Belk](/wiki/Belk "Belk"), [JCPenney](/wiki/JCPenney "JCPenney"), and [Dillard's](/wiki/Dillard%27s "Dillard's")), a 14-screen movie theater, a food court, and roughly 170 stores in total.
* [The Market Common](/wiki/The_Market_Common "The Market Common") is a [lifestyle district](/wiki/Lifestyle_center_(retail) "Lifestyle center (retail)") housing several upscale retail and apartments. It is on the site of the former [Myrtle Beach Air Force Base](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_Air_Force_Base "Myrtle Beach Air Force Base").
* [Myrtle Beach Mall](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_Mall "Myrtle Beach Mall") is 525,385 square feet (48,800 m2), and has three anchor stores, [Bass Pro Shops](/wiki/Bass_Pro_Shops "Bass Pro Shops"), [Belk](/wiki/Belk "Belk") and [JCPenney](/wiki/JCPenney "JCPenney"). The single-story mall also has a 12-screen movie theater, a food court, and other specialty stores. It used to be known as Colonial Mall, and was built as Briarcliffe Mall.
* [Tanger Outlets](/wiki/Tanger_Outlets "Tanger Outlets") is a shopping outlet center. There are two locations, one near [Briarcliffe Acres](/wiki/Briarcliffe_Acres,_South_Carolina "Briarcliffe Acres, South Carolina") and one near [Carolina Forest](/wiki/Carolina_Forest,_South_Carolina "Carolina Forest, South Carolina").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

### Golf

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Golf")]

The area is home to numerous golf courses and mini-golf courses along the Grand Strand and further inland. Myrtle Beach has been called the "Golf Capital of the World"[[68]](#cite_note-Vern-68) because of the roughly 100 golf courses there, the record 4.2 million rounds played, and many miniature golf courses.[[68]](#cite_note-Vern-68) 3.2 million total rounds of golf were played in 2017, down a "few hundred rounds" from a decade earlier. The number of golf courses more than doubled to over 120 over a 20-year period before declining late in the first decade of the 21st century. [Tiger Woods](/wiki/Tiger_Woods "Tiger Woods") declared Myrtle Beach "the mecca of golf" when visiting in 1997.[[69]](#cite_note-bestler-69) The majority of the area's golf courses are public. The Grand Strand is home of "[Hootie and the Blowfish](/wiki/Hootie_and_the_Blowfish "Hootie and the Blowfish") Day After the Masters Tournament". Extensive Chinese investment to the Myrtle Beach area golf market has resulted in a significant surge of Chinese tourists to the area and also resulted in 25+ golf courses being owned and/or operated by Chinese nationals.

Some of the notable golf courses and/or resorts include [Barefoot Resort](/wiki/Barefoot_Resort "Barefoot Resort") & Golf and [TPC of Myrtle Beach](/wiki/TPC_of_Myrtle_Beach "TPC of Myrtle Beach").

### Long-distance running events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Long-distance running events")]

From 1998 to 2009 and again starting in 2011 (no Saturday races were held in 2010 due to snow), the area hosted the *[Bi-Lo](/wiki/BI-LO_(United_States) "BI-LO (United States)") Myrtle Beach Marathon presented by [Chick-Fil-A](/wiki/Chick-Fil-A "Chick-Fil-A")*, every February featuring (since 2004) the Friday night [Royal Bank of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Bank_of_Canada "Royal Bank of Canada") 5K and the Saturday Dasani Half Marathon and Bi-Lo Marathon (from 1998 until 2008, a relay was held but dropped because of the popularity of the other events). Marathon day draws the limit of 6,000 runners annually (2,500 full, 3,500 half) and results usually in an unusual dawn as the race starts before dawn (6:30 am) in order to finish by 2:30 pm.

### Athletic teams and facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Athletic teams and facilities")]

Myrtle Beach is home to the [Myrtle Beach Pelicans](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_Pelicans "Myrtle Beach Pelicans"), a [Carolina League](/wiki/Carolina_League "Carolina League") baseball team and a [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") farm franchise. It was also home to the [Myrtle Beach Mutiny](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_Mutiny "Myrtle Beach Mutiny"), a pro soccer team playing in the [Premier Development League](/wiki/Premier_Development_League "Premier Development League") until 2018.

[Pelicans Ballpark](/wiki/Pelicans_Ballpark "Pelicans Ballpark") is the home field of the Myrtle Beach Pelicans. It opened in 1999. It is the finish point of the Bi-Lo Myrtle Beach Marathon. Pelicans Ballpark is also home of the annual "Baseball at the Beach" collegiate baseball tournament. Hosted by [Coastal Carolina University](/wiki/Coastal_Carolina_University "Coastal Carolina University") each year, the tournament pits participating NCAA Division I baseball programs in the United States.

### NASCAR

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: NASCAR")]

[NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR")-sanctioned [Stock car racing](/wiki/Stock_car_racing "Stock car racing") was held at [Myrtle Beach Speedway](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_Speedway "Myrtle Beach Speedway"), a .538-mile (866 m), semi-banked, asphalt-paved oval track lon [US 501](/wiki/US_501 "US 501"). Drivers in the Late Model classes will compete (against those of [Greenville-Pickens Speedway](/wiki/Greenville-Pickens_Speedway "Greenville-Pickens Speedway")) for the South Carolina Championship in the [NASCAR Whelen All-American Series](/wiki/NASCAR_Whelen_All-American_Series "NASCAR Whelen All-American Series"). South Carolina Champions' scores will be calculated against other state and provincial champions for a continental championship. It hosted the 2010 UOA Nationals where 8 collegiate ultimate teams from 5 conferences would be represented.

On August 15, 2020, the track held its final race, as the track was closed and demolished soon after, it is now the site of new townhomes.

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Government")]

The city of Myrtle Beach has a [council-manager government](/wiki/Council-manager_government "Council-manager government").[[70]](#cite_note-70) The Mayor sits as a Council member-at-large and presides over [City council](/wiki/City_council "City council") meetings. If the Mayor cannot preside over a City Council meeting, the Mayor Pro-Tem is the [presiding officer](/wiki/Chairman "Chairman") of the meeting until such time as the Mayor returns to the seat. The City Manager is responsible for the administration and the day-to-day operation of all of the [municipal services](/wiki/Municipal_services "Municipal services") and city departments. The City Manager also maintains intergovernmental relationships with federal, state, county and other local governments. The city of Myrtle Beach reformed into a council-manager government system in 1974 under the administration of Mayor Bob Hirsch and city manager David Stradinger, who moved away from the city's former [strong-mayor form](/wiki/Strong_mayor "Strong mayor") of government.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### Newspapers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Newspapers")]

*[The Sun News](/wiki/The_Sun_News "The Sun News")* has traditionally been the largest daily paper published along the [Grand Strand](/wiki/Grand_Strand "Grand Strand"), with a readership base extending from [Georgetown, South Carolina](/wiki/Georgetown,_South_Carolina "Georgetown, South Carolina"), to [Sunset Beach, North Carolina](/wiki/Sunset_Beach,_North_Carolina "Sunset Beach, North Carolina"). The paper has been in existence since the 1930s and was formerly published by [Knight Ridder](/wiki/Knight_Ridder "Knight Ridder") before that company was bought by [The McClatchy Company](/wiki/The_McClatchy_Company "The McClatchy Company"). Like many newspapers, the paper has declined in recent years due to shifts in readership and financial pressures at its parent company. It stopped local printing operations and downsized its newsroom in May 2020.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

Myrtle Beach is also served by *[The Myrtle Beach Herald](/wiki/The_Myrtle_Beach_Herald "The Myrtle Beach Herald")*, a weekly newspaper that is part of the Waccamaw Publishers group. *The Herald* also produces a newspaper targeted at tourists called  *Visit!*.

In 2020, Charleston-based *[The Post and Courier](/wiki/The_Post_and_Courier "The Post and Courier")* set up an official bureau in Myrtle Beach, after years of attempting to move into the market and purchasing *The Georgetown Times*, which it merged with the new operation.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

Grand Strand Medical Center (GSMC) is a 369-bed acute care hospital and Level 1 Adult Trauma Center known for its programs in [cardiology](/wiki/Cardiology "Cardiology"), [heart surgery](/wiki/Heart_surgery "Heart surgery"), and stroke treatment. It was opened on 21 April 1978 to succeed the former Ocean View Memorial Hospital (1958–1978), the first major hospital in Myrtle Beach. GSMC contains the only [cardiac surgery](/wiki/Cardiac_surgery "Cardiac surgery") and [neurosurgery](/wiki/Neurosurgery "Neurosurgery") programs in the greater Myrtle Beach area and was a recipient of the [Healthgrades](/wiki/Healthgrades "Healthgrades") 100 Best Hospitals for Cardiac Surgery in 2012 and 2013. As a teaching hospital, Grand Strand Medical Center is the home of [ACGME](/wiki/ACGME "ACGME") accredited [residency training programs](/wiki/Residency_training_program "Residency training program") ([internal medicine](/wiki/Internal_medicine "Internal medicine"), [general surgery](/wiki/General_surgery "General surgery"), [emergency medicine](/wiki/Emergency_medicine "Emergency medicine"), and [family medicine](/wiki/Family_medicine "Family medicine")), and hosts rotating medical students from the [University of South Carolina](/wiki/University_of_South_Carolina "University of South Carolina") and [Edward Via College of Osteopathic Medicine](/wiki/Edward_Via_College_of_Osteopathic_Medicine "Edward Via College of Osteopathic Medicine") (VCOM). Over 270 physicians and 1,400 staff serve at the facility.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)

#### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Air")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Myr-marketcommon-commerce.jpg/250px-Myr-marketcommon-commerce.jpg)](/wiki/File:Myr-marketcommon-commerce.jpg)

[The Market Common](/wiki/The_Market_Common "The Market Common") was once the location of Myrtle Beach AFB.

[Myrtle Beach International Airport](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_International_Airport "Myrtle Beach International Airport") is a county-owned public-use airport three miles (4.8 km) southwest of the central business district of Myrtle Beach. It was formerly known as Myrtle Beach Jetport (1974–1989) and is on the site of the former [Myrtle Beach Air Force Base](/wiki/Myrtle_Beach_Air_Force_Base "Myrtle Beach Air Force Base"). The 11,500-square-foot (1,070 m2) general aviation terminal, opened in May 2010, is located on the opposite side of the airport on Airdrome Street, near [The Market Common](/wiki/The_Market_Common "The Market Common"). The terminal replaced a building previously used as base operations for the Myrtle Beach Air Force Base.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

#### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Rail")]

The [Waccamaw Coast Line Railroad](/wiki/Waccamaw_Coast_Line_Railroad "Waccamaw Coast Line Railroad") is a 14.1-mile (22.7 km) [short-line railroad](/wiki/Short-line_railroad "Short-line railroad") division of the [Baltimore and Annapolis Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Annapolis_Railroad "Baltimore and Annapolis Railroad"),[[79]](#cite_note-79) extending from a connection with the [Carolina Southern Railroad](/wiki/Carolina_Southern_Railroad "Carolina Southern Railroad"), another division of that company, at Conway to Myrtle Beach. The line was opened in 1900 by the [Conway Coast and Western Railroad](/wiki/Conway_Coast_and_Western_Railroad "Conway Coast and Western Railroad"), a predecessor of the [Atlantic Coast Line Railroad](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Line_Railroad "Atlantic Coast Line Railroad").[[80]](#cite_note-80) The [Seaboard System Railroad](/wiki/Seaboard_System_Railroad "Seaboard System Railroad") sold the line to [Horry County](/wiki/Horry_County,_South_Carolina "Horry County, South Carolina") in November 1985, and it was operated by the [Horry County Railway](/wiki/Horry_County_Railway "Horry County Railway") until October 1987, when the WCLR took over.[[81]](#cite_note-shortline-81) The Carolina Southern Railroad acquired the WCLR in September 1995, and since then it has been a division of the Baltimore and Annapolis Railroad.[[82]](#cite_note-82) Horry County owns the line but leased it to the Carolina Southern Railroad in 2000.

[Carolina Southern Railroad](/wiki/Carolina_Southern_Railroad "Carolina Southern Railroad") is a short line rail operator running on less than 95 miles (153 km) of rail at a maximum speed of 10 mph (16 km/h). It transports mostly freight brought to it from national rail operators. The company makes one scheduled delivery per month into the City of Myrtle Beach.[[83]](#cite_note-83) It is off of Main Street in Conway, South Carolina, and is one of the few remaining train depots in South Carolina. It has been painstakingly restored to its former glory and the Carolina Southern Railroad has become one of the frequent destinations for freight services as well as passenger cars and observational locomotives. The railroad was originally erected in late 1886 and the first train steamed into the Conway Depot in December 1887.

The Carolina Southern Railroad stands as a permanent landmark in Southern History. Carolina Southern Railroad is a member of the Carolina Rails system with connections that run from [Whiteville, North Carolina](/wiki/Whiteville,_North_Carolina "Whiteville, North Carolina"), to [Mullins, South Carolina](/wiki/Mullins,_South_Carolina "Mullins, South Carolina"), and also from [Chadbourn, North Carolina](/wiki/Chadbourn,_North_Carolina "Chadbourn, North Carolina"), to Conway. Carolina Southern railroad is also responsible for operation of the Waccamaw Coast Line Railroad, which is a railway that runs from Conway to Myrtle Beach.

On August 30, 2011, Carolina Southern Railroad voluntary shut down because several bridges along the rail were overdue for maintenance. The shutdown caused Carolina Southern Railroad to lay off nearly all of its employees. On May 24, 2012, the Federal Railroad Administration commenced a bridge inspection on the CSR and the Waccamaw Coastline Railroad (the Horry County portion of rail). Fifty-two of 187 bridges were inspected and seven had critical defects. On May 26, 2011, the CSR voluntarily ceased operation, so that they could bring in a certified bridge engineer to inspect the seven critical bridges and prepare a plan for their repairs. Based on the engineer's recommendations, CSR made the necessary repairs on the bridges and CSR resumed operations August 8, 2011. The FRA returned to inspect the bridges and made a recommendation that CSR cease operations until all bridge repairs were completed.[[84]](#cite_note-Horry_County_South_Carolina_and_Carolina_Southern_Railroad_Infrastructure_Project_2012-84) There are efforts to bring the railroad back online.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-Carolina_Southern_Railroad_Revitalization_Project-Cost_Estimate-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)

In 2015, railroad operator RJ Corman acquired the former CSR line and re-opened it freight service in early April 2016.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/South_Carolina_31.svg/40px-South_Carolina_31.svg.png) [SC 31](/wiki/South_Carolina_Highway_31 "South Carolina Highway 31")
* [Farrow Parkway](/wiki/Farrow_Parkway "Farrow Parkway")
* [Grissom Parkway](/wiki/Robert_Grissom_Parkway "Robert Grissom Parkway")
* [Harrelson Boulevard](/wiki/Harrelson_Boulevard "Harrelson Boulevard")
* [Ocean Boulevard](/wiki/South_Carolina_Highway_73 "South Carolina Highway 73")

Within the last decade, new roads have been created to ease congestion caused by the yearly influx of visitors. Most of these roads follow the Metro Loop Road Plan, organized in 1997 to improve the traffic flow of Myrtle Beach. Some of the roads included have either been funded through [Road Improvement Development Effort](/wiki/Road_Improvement_Development_Effort "Road Improvement Development Effort") (RIDE I) funding or through the City of Myrtle Beach.

RIDE II plans include the third phase of [SC 31](/wiki/South_Carolina_Highway_31 "South Carolina Highway 31"), a graded separation of [Farrow Parkway](/wiki/Farrow_Parkway "Farrow Parkway") and US 17 Bypass at the back gate of the former Air Force base, and many other projects. The county is debating where to allocate the $400 million generated through a proposed 1-cent sales tax.[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99) Other road projects in Horry County, including some in [Aynor](/wiki/Aynor,_South_Carolina "Aynor, South Carolina") and Conway, will be included when voted upon.

Plans exist for Myrtle Beach to be served by two interstates, [I-73](/wiki/Interstate_73 "Interstate 73") and [I-74](/wiki/Interstate_74 "Interstate 74"). The [Robert Edge Parkway](/wiki/Robert_Edge_Parkway "Robert Edge Parkway") will connect I-74 to downtown [North Myrtle Beach](/wiki/North_Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina "North Myrtle Beach, South Carolina").

#### Mass transit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Myrtle_Beach,_South_Carolina&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Mass transit")]

Myrtle Beach is served by the [Coast RTA](/wiki/Coast_RTA "Coast RTA") and the [Pee Dee Regional Transportation Authority](/wiki/Pee_Dee_Regional_Transportation_Authority "Pee Dee Regional Transportation Authority").[[100]](#cite_note-100)
